channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: Jason turned $11 and one tweet into 1.1M views | E2311
gist: Jason Calacanis and Lon Harris host serial founder Tibo Lucas on the 'build in public' audience-compounding playbook, then dig into the Apple v. OpenAI trade-secrets lawsuit, the economics and policy fights around autonomous-vehicle rollout in the US and China, an economist-signed warning on AI job displacement, and Flock Safety's surveillance-camera PR crisis.

insights.claim: Tibo Lucas built Tweet Hunter as his 11th public product launch within four months, with each earlier public launch compounding his Twitter following until Tweet Hunter itself surpassed $1M ARR within a year and was acquired by Lemlist.
insights.speaker: Tibo Lucas (guest, serial founder)
insights.so_what: Serial public shipping compounds audience/distribution faster than betting everything on one launch — a repeatable, low-cost customer-acquisition strategy other founders can copy.
insights.quote: the 11th product was Tweet Hunter. That was a hit like at the very very beginning. In less than a year, it reached a million per year and got eventually sold to to List.

insights.claim: Tibo Lucas's growth strategy was to target one narrow audience (creator-economy Twitter/LinkedIn users) and have every new product solve a different problem for that same audience, so the following built by an earlier product carried over to the next launch.
insights.speaker: Tibo Lucas (guest)
insights.so_what: A repeatable 'same audience, new product' framework for multi-product founders — avoid resetting distribution with every launch.

insights.claim: Tibo Lucas shifted influencer marketing from paying a handful of large flat sponsorship fees toward seeding hundreds of micro-influencers a small base fee (around $200) plus a success bonus (around $500) once their content breaks 50,000 views, then re-investing in whichever partnerships convert.
insights.speaker: Tibo Lucas (guest)
insights.so_what: A capital-efficient influencer playbook — pay for outcomes across many small bets rather than for guaranteed reach, since algorithmic distribution now matters more than follower count.
insights.quote: suggest a base fee of something like 200 USD. Uh, and then if they break something like a 50k views on their content, talking talking about my product, uh, I would offer a bonus of about 500.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis argues that platform 'for you' algorithms have replaced follower count as the real distribution currency — even accounts with over a million followers get no reach if the algorithm decides a given post isn't engaging.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Reframes influencer-marketing ROI: pay for algorithmic breakout potential across many small creator bets instead of buying guaranteed reach from big accounts.
insights.quote: now even if you've got a million followers if grock is like people aren't going to be interested in this particular tweet, you don't get shared on the for you and people don't see it.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis built a personal podcast-highlights tool ('Pod Me') for about $11 using Perplexity Comet as the agent harness and Grok 4.5 to organize deep-linked segments across up to 75 podcasts, and a tweet about it drew 1.1 million views.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Shows how cheap AI agent tooling has become for building niche utilities — sub-$15 builds can generate outsized organic reach when shared publicly, reinforcing the build-in-public thesis.
insights.quote: It cost me $11. It has now 1.1 million views this tweet.

insights.claim: LinkedIn actively blocks AI browser agents such as Perplexity's Comet or Claude's browser from searching or contacting people on its platform, pushing power users toward third-party scraping tools built in countries with weaker data-scraping regulation such as Israel, the Philippines, and India.
insights.speaker: Tibo Lucas / Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: A regulatory/enforcement arbitrage is forming around AI-agent data access — offshore scraping tools are becoming the workaround as major platforms lock down agentic browsing.
insights.quote: if you use the Comet browser from Perplexity or any of the smart browsers or you try to use your browser from claude to like search for people to do anything, they try to kill it immediately.

insights.claim: Apple filed a 40-page lawsuit against OpenAI alleging a systematic effort to acquire confidential information, including encouraging Apple employees to study confidential materials ahead of OpenAI interviews and to bring hardware components and prototypes with them.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris (host)
insights.so_what: Sets up potential injunctive relief against OpenAI's hardware ambitions and a high-profile trade-secrets precedent to track alongside the earlier Waymo v. Uber/Otto case.
insights.quote: Apple last week filed a 40-page lawsuit against Open AI, alleging that the company made a systematic effort to acquire and use confidential information to help them replicate Apple's consumer electronics business.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis believes Apple would not have filed this trade-secrets suit unless it had strong evidence, comparing the situation to the earlier Waymo v. Uber case where the accused engineer was criminally convicted.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Investors should weight litigation risk to OpenAI's hardware plans (with Jony Ive) as elevated rather than treating the suit as a nuisance claim.
insights.quote: you don't have this happen, these lawsuits, unless it is they have them dead to rights.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis argues OpenAI made a major strategic error by pursuing Apple hardware talent (including former Apple design executive Tang Tan) and damaging the ChatGPT-as-default-iPhone-assistant relationship, ceding that opportunity to Google, which he says is now making roughly $1 billion a year from its Gemini-Apple deal.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete opportunity-cost thesis on OpenAI's platform strategy — the lost Apple default-assistant deal is now a recurring ~$1B/year revenue line for Google, worth tracking in Alphabet's disclosures as a proxy for what OpenAI forfeited.
insights.quote: Google now is making a billion dollars a year off of the Apple relationship. They're doing Gemini. So, they lost the Chad GBT deal because of this.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis speculates that Jony Ive's OpenAI equity (from OpenAI's roughly $6.4 billion all-equity acquisition of his hardware startup) could now be worth more than his lifetime Apple equity, given OpenAI's rising valuation.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A speculative but directional signal of how much paper wealth has shifted from Apple's design organization to OpenAI's hardware bet — worth checking against actual OpenAI cap-table disclosures if they surface in litigation discovery.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis frames Sam Altman's pattern of major lawsuits (against Elon Musk, The New York Times, and now Apple) as 'enemies accumulating,' arguing these disputes could have been resolved as partnerships, and warns that once a hot company stumbles, accumulated ill will invites retaliation.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A reputational-risk framework for evaluating founder-led litigation exposure — watch for pressure on OpenAI to settle or restructure partnerships as a signal of weakening leverage if growth slows.
insights.quote: Enemies accumulate. Be very very careful. And this is I think what Sam's learned.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis predicts Apple will pursue an injunction forcing OpenAI to halt or reverse elements of a competing hardware product and to stop poaching Apple employees, rather than settle quietly.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Watch for a court-ordered delay to OpenAI's hardware device timeline as a near-term catalyst/risk for the AI-hardware race.
insights.quote: I think they're going to go after the injunction which is you can't release a product and you got to like reverse this.

themes.theme: Autonomous Vehicle Economics and Rollout Timeline

insights.claim: A newly proposed Washington DC bill would update the district's 2012 Autonomous Vehicle Act to allow fully driverless commercial robotaxi operation without a human safety operator; Waymo supports the bill while Uber opposes it in favor of a hybrid model mixing AV and human-driven rides.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term regulatory catalyst to watch — the DC hearing outcome could signal how quickly other municipalities follow with driverless-commercial legislation.

insights.claim: China suspended new autonomous-vehicle licenses as of late April 2026, according to Reuters, after protests from roughly 10 million cab drivers facing about 10% downward pricing pressure from unrestricted robotaxi rollout across 20 providers in 10 cities.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A leading indicator for US policy — labor-driven pushback tends to force AV licensing caps once rollout accelerates, even under a regulatory system with more central authority.
insights.quote: China has suspended, this is from Reuters. What's the date on this one? April 29th, 2026.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis estimates ride-hailing is only about 1-2% of all US rides today, and of that, roughly 0.2-0.5% are currently autonomous (a figure he sourced from Gemini), meaning AVs are a small fraction of a small fraction of total US personal transportation.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Puts current AV hype in context — even under bullish doubling assumptions it takes years to meaningfully shift total ride volume, tempering near-term disruption timelines for rideshare/delivery labor and incumbent platform economics.
insights.quote: 1 to 2% of all rides in the US currently are ride shares and 0.2 to 5%, this is according to Gemini, 0.2 to 0.5% of those ride shares are in AVs currently.

insights.claim: Assuming the AV share of US rides roughly doubles each year from today's sub-1% base, Jason Calacanis projects about 30% AV ride penetration by 2030 and roughly 50% by 2032-2033.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete, falsifiable timeline investors can track against actual Waymo/Tesla/Zoox ride volume data to judge whether AV disruption is running ahead of or behind schedule.
insights.quote: I am going to make two predictions. 50% of rides are um automated 2032.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis assigns greater than 50% odds that a viral video of an AV-caused fatality or serious injury triggers a one-to-two-year regulatory pause in autonomous-vehicle rollout, even though AVs are statistically safer than human drivers, who cause roughly 30,000 US deaths per year.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Identifies a tail-risk catalyst — a single viral incident could abruptly stall AV-exposed stocks (Tesla, Uber, Lyft) regardless of underlying safety statistics, a scenario worth monitoring via incident news flow.
insights.quote: I think there is a very significant chance that all of this gets paused if just two people get killed

insights.claim: Uber, Lyft, and DoorDash are managing what Jason Calacanis calls a 'civil war' transition — investing heavily in AV and robotics (Uber alone has put $10 billion into self-driving companies) while simultaneously supporting driver-protective legislation to slow the pace of full automation and retain their human workforce during the transition.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Explains an apparent contradiction in gig-economy platform behavior (pro-AV capex, anti-fast-rollout lobbying) as rational transition-risk management rather than hypocrisy — relevant to reading these platforms' AV capex and lobbying spend.
insights.quote: Uber has put10 billion dollars into self-driving companies. So they're they know that this is happening.

insights.claim: Tesla is positioned as the outlier pushing for federal preemption of state and local AV regulation, seeking a single national rulebook rather than the localized, city-by-city licensing approach Jason Calacanis expects to actually prevail in the US.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Suggests Tesla's regulatory strategy is structurally mismatched with how US transportation regulation actually works (state/local control), a potential headwind for its robotaxi ambitions relative to city-approved players like Waymo.
insights.quote: that would be Tesla, right? Tesla wants federal legislation. They want to override every state's ability, every city's ability to make their own decisions.

themes.theme: AI Job Displacement and Transition Policy

insights.claim: An open letter titled 'We Must Act Now,' signed by roughly 200 economists including 15 Nobel laureates plus the chief economists of OpenAI and Anthropic, Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark, former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, and venture capitalist Vinod Khosla, warns that AI will radically transform the US economy and calls for a plan to offset millions of potential white-collar job losses during the transition.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris (host)
insights.so_what: A signal that AI-industry insiders now publicly acknowledge disruptive near-term labor impact — relevant to policy risk (automation taxes, transition funds) that could raise the cost of AI adoption for enterprises.
insights.quote: 200 economists including 15 Noble laureates published an open letter today. It's called we must act now.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis supports an 'AI unemployment fund' concept — an incremental tax on companies (he floats an extra 5%) when they replace a human job with AI, modeled on existing state unemployment-insurance contributions, to fund support for displaced workers.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A specific policy mechanism that, if adopted, would raise the effective cost of AI-driven headcount reduction for enterprises — worth modeling into automation ROI cases as regulatory risk.
insights.quote: you put like an extra 5% in the in the tank for other people who are getting laid off like I like ideas like that.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis supports phased, state-level licensing caps on autonomous-vehicle rollout (roughly doubling the permitted share each year) funded by license fees earmarked for displaced-driver unemployment support, describing it as a workable transition mechanism.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Another concrete policy lever (fee-funded phased licensing) that could shape the pace and cost of AV rollout for platform operators — worth tracking as state legislatures take up similar bills.

insights.claim: Flock Safety CEO Garrett Langley calling opponents of his license-plate-camera network 'terrorists' (in response to the open-source Deflock camera-mapping project) backfired, and Jason Calacanis argues it mirrors past founder PR mistakes, such as Uber's Travis Kalanick dismissing surge-pricing critics, that inflame rather than defuse legitimate concerns.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A reusable crisis-comms framework for evaluating portfolio companies facing backlash: dismissive, hyperbolic responses to legitimate criticism escalate controversy rather than containing it.
insights.quote: Flock is doing good work, but their comms are suboptimal at best. I've seen this movie before with Robin Hood and Uber and it's an easy fix.

insights.claim: Ring reportedly cut off its data-sharing collaboration with Flock Safety after backlash to a Super Bowl ad implying doorbell-camera footage would help 'spy on' neighbors, illustrating how partner platforms distance themselves from a surveillance company's PR failures.
insights.speaker: Lon Harris (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete example of reputational contagion risk in B2B surveillance/data partnerships — a partner's PR crisis can force other companies to sever integrations regardless of product legality.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis argues Flock Safety could defuse privacy backlash by publicly committing to specific, auditable policies — no facial recognition, a 12-month data-retention cap, mandatory officer badge-ID logging for every license-plate lookup, and installation only in communities with roughly 75% or greater support — rather than dismissing critics.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A specific transparency playbook (retention caps, audit logs, community consent thresholds) that surveillance-adjacent companies could adopt to reduce regulatory and reputational risk — relevant to governance diligence on similar security-tech portfolio companies.
insights.quote: We do not allow facial recognition. We do not take pictures of that and all data is gone in 12 months.

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis notes he personally runs an extensive private surveillance setup (Unifi facial recognition and license-plate readers across multiple properties, retained indefinitely), illustrating that wealthy individuals already deploy the same surveillance capability that draws public backlash when done by companies like Flock Safety.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Highlights an access/equity gap in surveillance tech — private, unregulated deployment by the wealthy versus regulated public deployment — a framing relevant to how surveillance-tech companies position themselves against privacy criticism.
insights.quote: I have facial recognition on all my properties. I have license plate readers on all my properties.
